---
title: "Case Studies"
subtitle: "Simulated epidemic data for cross-disciplinary analysis"
---

## Overview

The workshop is structured around two case studies derived from a single simulated population-wide outbreak. The underlying data generation mechanism is broadly inspired by the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic and uses a structured compartmental SEIRD (Susceptible–Exposed–Infected–Recovered–Dead) model.

::: {.callout-important}
Participants do **not** see the underlying complete individual-level dataset until the very end of the workshop. The two case study datasets are extracts designed to mimic realistic data availability settings.
:::

---

## Underlying Data Generation

**Population:** 1,000,000 individuals in a closed, structured population with within-cluster and between-cluster infection dynamics.

**Model:** Compartmental SEIRD with:

- Heterogeneity in susceptibility to infection
- Heterogeneity in infectiousness upon infection
- A temporary **intervention period** that reduces the transmission rate and effective reproduction number (R~eff~)
- A **vaccination campaign** that begins at a later calendar time point, with effectiveness depending on individual characteristics and time since vaccination

**Individual-level variables** (complete dataset, not shared until Day 5):

- Infection status (yes/no)
- Time of infection (if infected)
- Time of vaccination (if vaccinated)
- Clinical events: hospitalization, discharge, death

---

## Case Study A — Natural Course of the Epidemic

**Focus period:** Before vaccination becomes available.

**Available data:** Daily aggregate counts of:

- New infections (subject to reporting delays and underreporting)
- Hospitalizations
- Deaths (including cause of death)

**Analytical aims:**

- Estimate the **reproduction number** (R~0~ and R~eff~) over time
- Estimate the **effect of intervention measures** on transmission
- **Predict the future course** of the epidemic under different scenarios
- Estimate **excess mortality** attributable to the outbreak
- Handle **incomplete data** — censoring, reporting delays, and underreporting

**Relevant methods:**

| Survival Analysis | Infectious Disease Modeling |
|---|---|
| Competing risks (infection, death) | Compartmental SIR/SEIRD models |
| Multi-state models (S → E → I → R/D) | Renewal equation estimation of R~eff~ |
| Additive hazard models | Back-calculation methods |
| Censoring adjustment | Nowcasting with reporting delay correction |

---

## Case Study B — Vaccine Efficacy

**Focus period:** After vaccination becomes available.

**Available data:** Daily aggregate counts (vaccination period only) of:

- New infections, hospitalizations, deaths
- Vaccinations (including vaccine uptake aggregated over population subgroups)

**Analytical aims:**

- Estimate **vaccine efficacy** (VE) against infection, hospitalization, and death
- Understand **time-dependent vaccine effectiveness** (waning immunity)
- Characterize **heterogeneity** in vaccine uptake and efficacy across subgroups
- Account for confounding by indication and time-varying exposures

**Relevant methods:**

| Survival Analysis | Infectious Disease Modeling |
|---|---|
| Frailty models (shared/individual-level heterogeneity) | Vaccine efficacy estimation from surveillance data |
| Time-varying covariate models | Cohort and case-control study designs |
| Multi-state models (unvaccinated → vaccinated → outcomes) | Mechanistic models of waning immunity |
| Competing risks (multiple clinical outcomes) | Indirect effects and herd immunity adjustment |

---

## Working Groups

Participants are divided into **four mixed-discipline groups** (two per case study), intentionally composed across:

- SA and IDM expertise
- Career stage (early-career and established researchers)

Each pair of groups working on the same case study meets on Day 2 for cross-group discussion before refining their analyses independently. All groups share findings in the daily plenary sessions.
